summary: O'Neil celebrates ProPublica's groundbreaking investigation into the COMPAS recidivism risk model, which revealed systematic racial bias in criminal sentencing algorithms. The analysis found that black defendants were nearly twice as likely to be falsely labeled high-risk, while white defendants were twice as likely to be incorrectly classified as low-risk. The study controlled for prior crimes, future recidivism, age, and gender, yet black defendants remained significantly more likely to receive higher risk scores. O'Neil argues this report validates concerns she and others have raised about algorithmic bias and sets a new standard for auditing such systems.
key_points: Black defendants who didn't reoffend were nearly twice as likely as white defendants to be misclassified as high-risk (45% vs 23%)
Even controlling for prior crimes, future recidivism, age, and gender, black defendants were 45-77% more likely to receive higher risk scores
ProPublica set a new transparency standard by publishing their methodology, data, and analysis code on GitHub
Recidivism risk models have been deployed 'naively' across judicial systems with scores presented as inherently fair
The report provides hard proof for concerns that algorithmic bias critics had been raising without concrete evidence
money_quotes: The piece explains in human terms what it looks like when algorithms are biased.
Black defendants who did not recidivate over a two-year period were nearly twice as likely to be misclassified as higher risk compared to their white counterparts.
This is a triumph for the community of people (like me!) who have been worrying about exactly this kind of thing but who haven't had hard proof until now.
The transparency involved is hugely important, because nobody will be able to say they don't know how these statistics were computed.

Until now, recidivism risk models have been deployed naively, in judicial systems all across the country, and judges in those systems have been presented with such scores as if they are inherently 'fair.'
People deploying these models will have pressure to make sure the models are audited for racism before using them.
key_insight: ProPublica's COMPAS investigation provides the first hard proof that recidivism risk algorithms systematically discriminate against black defendants, setting a new standard for algorithmic accountability.
